clinicalStem: A 58-year-old diabetic man presents to the ED with a 12-hour history of severe pain and swelling in his left leg that he describes as 'out of proportion' to the visible skin changes. He is febrile at 39.8°C, HR 118 bpm, BP 94/62 mmHg. Examination reveals erythema spreading rapidly from the knee to mid-thigh, with blistering and a small necrotic patch. Skin crepitus is palpable. WBC 22 x10⁹/L, CRP 380 mg/L, Na 130 mmol/L, creatinine 195 µmol/L. Blood cultures are pending. What is the most critical immediate next step in his management?
options: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and reassess in 4 hours
Arrange urgent CT scan of the leg to confirm diagnosis before any intervention
Initiate fluid resuscitation, give IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and arrange emergency surgical debridement within the hour
Perform bedside wound swab and await sensitivities before starting antibiotics
Admit to dermatology ward and start IV flucloxacillin for cellulitis
explanation: This presentation — pain out of proportion to skin findings, rapidly spreading erythema, skin crepitus, blistering, necrosis, and systemic sepsis in a diabetic — is necrotising fasciitis until proven otherwise. This is a surgical emergency with a mortality that rises sharply with every hour of delay. Immediate resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and anaerobes), and emergency surgical debridement within the hour are the cornerstones of management. CT can be helpful but must not delay theatre if clinical suspicion is high.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassessing in 4 hours is dangerous; necrotising fasciitis progresses in hours and delay significantly increases mortality.
CT scan may support the diagnosis (showing gas in soft tissues) but should not delay emergency surgery in a haemodynamically unstable patient with high clinical suspicion.
Correct — immediate resuscitation, antibiotics, and emergency surgical debridement are all required urgently.
Awaiting wound swab sensitivities before starting antibiotics is inappropriate in septic shock; empirical broad-spectrum therapy must be started immediately.
This presentation is not consistent with simple cellulitis; the crepitus, necrosis, and systemic shock indicate necrotising fasciitis requiring surgical management.
clinicalPearl: Pain out of proportion to visible skin findings plus crepitus in a febrile, systemically unwell patient = necrotising fasciitis; do not wait for imaging — take to theatre.
keyClue: Skin crepitus, pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading necrosis, and septic shock in a diabetic
commonExamTrap: Choosing antibiotics alone or waiting for CT before surgical referral — both delay the life-saving debridement.
missedConcept: Necrotising fasciitis requires surgical debridement as the definitive treatment; antibiotics alone are insufficient.
redFlagReason: Necrotising fasciitis with septic shock — delay in surgical debridement is directly associated with increased mortality.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old nurse presents with a 3-week history of intensely itchy, weeping, vesicular rash on both hands. The rash appeared shortly after she started using a new brand of latex-free gloves at work. There is no rash elsewhere. She has tried moisturiser with no improvement. On examination, vesicles and lichenification are confined to the dorsum of both hands and wrists. Patch testing is arranged but will take 3 weeks. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5 days
Potent topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1%) applied to the affected hands, combined with emollient and avoidance of the trigger
Topical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus 0.1%) as first-line monotherapy
Intramuscular triamcinolone injection
Antihistamine alone (cetirizine 10 mg daily) until patch test results are available
explanation: Allergic or irritant contact dermatitis on the hands from an occupational trigger is best managed with a potent topical corticosteroid (hands are a thick-skinned site requiring a potent agent), regular emollients, and strict avoidance of the offending trigger. Patch testing confirms the allergen but management should not be delayed. Oral steroids are reserved for severe or widespread cases not responding to topical therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral prednisolone is appropriate for severe, widespread, or recalcitrant contact dermatitis, not as first-line for localised hand dermatitis.
Correct — a potent topical steroid, emollient, and trigger avoidance form the first-line treatment.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors are second-line agents used when steroid-sparing therapy is needed; they are not first-line monotherapy for acute hand dermatitis.
IM triamcinolone is not a standard first-line choice and bypasses simpler topical options.
Antihistamines address itching symptomatically but do not treat the dermatitis; they are insufficient as sole management.
clinicalPearl: Hand contact dermatitis requires a potent topical steroid (not mild/moderate) because palmar/dorsal skin is thick; emollient and trigger avoidance are equally essential.
keyClue: Vesicular hand rash temporally linked to a new glove brand at work
commonExamTrap: Using a mild steroid (hydrocortisone 1%) on the hands — hands require a potent steroid due to skin thickness.
missedConcept: Potency matching: thick-skinned or lichenified sites need potent steroids; mild steroids are underpowered for hand dermatitis.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with known plaque psoriasis presents to the emergency department after abruptly stopping his methotrexate 2 weeks ago on the advice of a friend. He now has diffuse erythema and scaling covering more than 90% of his body surface area. He is febrile (38.6°C), tachycardic (HR 104 bpm), and has bilateral pitting oedema. He complains of feeling cold despite a warm room. Blood tests show hypoalbuminaemia (21 g/L) and a raised WBC. What is the most important immediate priority in his management?
options: Restart methotrexate at the previous dose immediately
Apply high-potency topical corticosteroids to the entire body surface
Admit to hospital, provide supportive care (IV fluids, warming, nutritional support, emollients), monitor for infection and cardiac failure, and involve dermatology urgently
Begin narrowband UVB phototherapy three times per week as an outpatient
Start oral prednisolone 60 mg daily to rapidly reduce skin inflammation
explanation: Erythrodermic psoriasis is a medical emergency. Loss of skin barrier function leads to massive fluid and protein loss (hypoalbuminaemia), thermoregulatory failure, and high risk of secondary infection and high-output cardiac failure. Immediate hospital admission for supportive care — IV fluid and electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, temperature regulation, emollients, and close monitoring — is the priority, with urgent dermatology involvement to guide safe systemic treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Methotrexate should not be restarted without full clinical reassessment; it is not the immediate priority in an unstable patient.
High-potency topical steroids over the entire body surface area are contraindicated due to systemic absorption risk and are not appropriate in erythroderma.
Correct — inpatient supportive management addressing fluid, protein, temperature, and infection risk is the cornerstone of initial care.
Phototherapy is contraindicated in erythrodermic psoriasis as it can exacerbate the condition.
Systemic corticosteroids are generally avoided in psoriasis as they can precipitate rebound pustular or erythrodermic psoriasis on withdrawal.
clinicalPearl: Erythrodermic psoriasis is a dermatological emergency: look for thermoregulatory failure, hypoalbuminaemia, and high-output cardiac failure — systemic steroids are contraindicated.
keyClue: BSA >90% erythema, fever, tachycardia, hypoalbuminaemia, and oedema following abrupt withdrawal of systemic therapy
commonExamTrap: Prescribing systemic corticosteroids — they can cause a rebound pustular flare when stopped and are avoided in psoriasis.
missedConcept: Erythroderma of any cause causes protein/fluid loss and thermoregulatory failure; supportive care takes priority over disease-specific therapy in the acute phase.
redFlagReason: Erythrodermic psoriasis can cause high-output cardiac failure, sepsis, and death — requires urgent inpatient management.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with recently diagnosed systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents to your clinic complaining that her facial rash worsens dramatically every summer. On examination, she has a confluent erythematous rash across both cheeks and the nasal bridge sparing the nasolabial folds. She is not on any treatment yet. Her ANA is strongly positive (1:640) with positive anti-dsDNA. Which of the following is the most important initial management step for her skin disease?
options: Start azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day immediately to prevent renal involvement
Advise strict photoprotection (broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen, UV-protective clothing, sun avoidance) and start hydroxychloroquine
Prescribe a superpotent topical steroid (clobetasol propionate) for daily use on the face
Arrange immediate renal biopsy to stratify lupus nephritis risk
Start oral prednisolone 40 mg daily as first-line therapy for cutaneous lupus
explanation: Cutaneous lupus is photosensitive; UV exposure triggers and exacerbates both skin and systemic flares. Strict photoprotection is essential. Hydroxychloroquine is the first-line systemic agent for cutaneous and systemic manifestations of SLE: it reduces flares, is steroid-sparing, and has a favourable safety profile. Together, these form the cornerstone of initial management for photosensitive SLE rash.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Azathioprine is a second-line steroid-sparing agent used for more severe systemic SLE; it is not the first management step for cutaneous disease.
Correct — photoprotection and hydroxychloroquine are the initial treatment priorities for photosensitive cutaneous SLE.
Superpotent topical steroids on the face risk skin atrophy, telangiectasia, and perioral dermatitis; facial use should use mild/moderate preparations if at all, and is not first-line here.
Renal biopsy is indicated when there is clinical or laboratory evidence of nephritis; it is not the priority for a patient presenting with isolated rash.
High-dose oral prednisolone is reserved for serious systemic lupus; cutaneous disease is managed with hydroxychloroquine and photoprotection first.
clinicalPearl: Hydroxychloroquine plus strict photoprotection is the first-line management for photosensitive cutaneous SLE and reduces systemic flare frequency.
keyClue: Malar (butterfly) rash that worsens in summer — photosensitivity pattern
commonExamTrap: Jumping to high-dose oral steroids or azathioprine before trying hydroxychloroquine, which is the first-line disease-modifying agent.
missedConcept: UV light is a key trigger for lupus flares; photoprotection is a treatment, not just advice.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of a painful vesicular rash on the right side of her forehead and tip of her nose. She reports blurred vision in the right eye and a gritty sensation. On examination, the rash follows the ophthalmic (V1) division of the trigeminal nerve. Vesicles are present on the tip and side of the nose (Hutchinson's sign). Her right conjunctiva is injected. What is the most urgent management priority?
options: Prescribe topical aciclovir cream to the skin lesions and advise review in 1 week
Start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily and reassure that ocular involvement is unlikely
Initiate high-dose oral antivirals (valaciclovir 1 g three times daily or aciclovir 800 mg five times daily) AND arrange same-day ophthalmology review
Admit for IV aciclovir and IV antibiotics empirically
Prescribe a short course of oral prednisolone to reduce post-herpetic neuralgia risk only
explanation: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) involves the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve. Hutchinson's sign (vesicles on the nose tip) indicates nasociliary nerve involvement, which predicts a high risk of ocular complications including keratitis, uveitis, and corneal scarring. Both systemic antivirals and same-day ophthalmology assessment are mandatory. Topical antiviral cream alone is insufficient and an oral antiviral without ophthalmology referral is incomplete management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical aciclovir cream has no role in treating HZO; systemic antivirals are required.
Oral antivirals are correct but reassuring that ocular involvement is unlikely is dangerous — Hutchinson's sign and conjunctival injection indicate high ocular risk.
Correct — systemic antivirals plus same-day ophthalmology is the mandatory dual-track management for HZO with Hutchinson's sign.
IV aciclovir is reserved for immunocompromised patients or those with severe/disseminated zoster; oral high-dose valaciclovir/aciclovir is appropriate here alongside ophthalmology review.
Steroids may be used adjunctively for ocular inflammation but only under ophthalmological supervision; prescribing steroids alone without antivirals is unsafe.
clinicalPearl: Hutchinson's sign (vesicles on the nose tip) in HZO = high risk of corneal and intraocular involvement — same-day ophthalmology referral is mandatory.
keyClue: Vesicles on the tip of the nose (Hutchinson's sign) with ipsilateral blurred vision and conjunctival injection in V1 distribution
commonExamTrap: Assuming oral antivirals alone are sufficient without ophthalmology referral, or missing the significance of Hutchinson's sign.
missedConcept: Nasociliary branch involvement in HZO (Hutchinson's sign) reliably predicts ocular complications and mandates urgent ophthalmology assessment.
redFlagReason: HZO with Hutchinson's sign risks sight-threatening corneal and intraocular complications if ophthalmology assessment and antivirals are delayed.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis has been on methotrexate 15 mg weekly for 8 months with good disease control. He attends for routine review. He is a non-drinker. His recent blood tests show: WBC 2.1 x10⁹/L, neutrophils 0.9 x10⁹/L, Hb 98 g/L, platelets 140 x10⁹/L, ALT 96 U/L (normal <40), creatinine normal. He feels well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue methotrexate at the current dose and recheck bloods in 3 months
Reduce the methotrexate dose by 2.5 mg and recheck in 4 weeks
Stop methotrexate immediately, withhold folic acid, and arrange urgent haematology review
Stop methotrexate immediately and arrange urgent full blood count, liver function review, and specialist dermatology/haematology input
Add folic acid 5 mg daily and continue methotrexate unchanged
explanation: This patient has methotrexate-induced bone marrow suppression (pancytopenia: low WBC, neutropenia, anaemia) and significant hepatotoxicity (ALT >2x upper limit of normal). Both are serious adverse effects requiring immediate cessation of methotrexate. The patient needs urgent haematology and hepatology/dermatology input. Neutropenia at this level increases infection risk. Folic acid supplementation should continue (it does not reverse marrow suppression but the drug must stop).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing methotrexate in the setting of significant bone marrow suppression and hepatotoxicity is unsafe and contraindicated.
Dose reduction is insufficient when there is already significant pancytopenia and hepatotoxicity; methotrexate must be stopped.
Withholding folic acid is incorrect — folic acid should continue; it is methotrexate that must stop.
Correct — immediate cessation of methotrexate with urgent haematology and specialist review is the appropriate action.
Increasing folic acid without stopping methotrexate will not correct bone marrow suppression and is dangerous.
clinicalPearl: Pancytopenia or significant transaminase elevation (>2x ULN) during methotrexate therapy mandates immediate drug cessation and urgent specialist review.
keyClue: Neutrophils 0.9 x10⁹/L, WBC 2.1, anaemia, and ALT 96 U/L on methotrexate — pancytopenia plus hepatotoxicity
commonExamTrap: Continuing or dose-reducing rather than stopping methotrexate when bone marrow toxicity is present.
missedConcept: Methotrexate causes dose-dependent bone marrow suppression and hepatotoxicity; regular monitoring is mandatory and toxicity requires immediate cessation.
redFlagReason: Methotrexate-induced neutropenia (0.9 x10⁹/L) creates serious risk of life-threatening infection; immediate drug cessation is required.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 4-month history of increasingly severe, greasy, yellowish scaling affecting the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and central chest. He reports it began as mild dandruff but has dramatically worsened. On further history, he has lost 6 kg unintentionally over 3 months and has recurrent oral ulcers. Examination confirms the seborrhoeic distribution with thick adherent scale. What is the single most important next investigation to arrange?
options: Skin biopsy of the scalp lesion
Patch testing to identify contact allergen
HIV serology (HIV-1/2 antibody/antigen test)
Serum zinc level
Fungal skin scrapings for Malassezia species
explanation: Severe, refractory, or atypically extensive seborrhoeic dermatitis in a young adult, especially combined with weight loss and recurrent oral ulcers, is a classic presentation prompting HIV testing. Seborrhoeic dermatitis is one of the most common and often first dermatological manifestations of HIV infection; its severity correlates with immunosuppression. HIV serology is the single most important investigation that will change overall management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Skin biopsy may show non-specific changes of seborrhoeic dermatitis and will not identify the underlying cause in this context.
Patch testing is used to identify allergens in contact dermatitis; it is not indicated here.
Correct — severe seborrhoeic dermatitis with systemic features (weight loss, oral ulcers) in a young adult mandates HIV testing.
Zinc deficiency can cause a seborrhoeic-like rash but does not explain the systemic features; zinc level is not the priority here.
Malassezia is implicated in seborrhoeic dermatitis pathogenesis, but this test will not address the underlying immunodeficiency suspected here.
clinicalPearl: Severe or treatment-resistant seborrhoeic dermatitis in a young adult + systemic features (weight loss, oral ulcers) = HIV must be excluded.
keyClue: Young adult, severe widespread seborrhoeic distribution, unintentional weight loss, and recurrent oral ulcers
commonExamTrap: Treating the skin condition without investigating the underlying cause in a patient with red flags for HIV.
missedConcept: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is a well-recognised marker of HIV/AIDS; systemic red flags should prompt HIV testing regardless of perceived risk.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student presents with a 3-week history of intense nocturnal itch affecting his finger webs, wrists, axillae, and groin. His roommate and girlfriend have similar symptoms. On examination, linear burrows are visible between the fingers. A clinical diagnosis of scabies is made. Which of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Treat the patient only; advise the contacts to seek their own GP appointment before treatment
Apply permethrin 5% cream to the patient from neck to toes overnight, and simultaneously treat all close contacts and household members on the same night, with washing of all bedding and clothing
Prescribe oral ivermectin as the only treatment required for the patient and suggest contacts use antihistamines for itch
Treat only the symptomatic contacts and advise the patient to use emollients until symptoms resolve
Apply permethrin to affected areas only (finger webs and wrists) and retreat if symptoms persist
explanation: Successful scabies management requires simultaneous treatment of all close contacts and household members on the same day/night to prevent reinfection, regardless of whether they are symptomatic. Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line topical treatment applied from neck to toes (including under nails) and left on for 8–12 hours. All bedding, clothing, and towels must be washed at 60°C or bagged for 72 hours. Failure to treat contacts simultaneously is the most common cause of treatment failure.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Treating the patient alone without simultaneous contact treatment will result in reinfestation; this approach is clinically incorrect.
Correct — simultaneous treatment of patient and all household/close contacts on the same night, plus environmental decontamination, is the standard management.
Oral ivermectin is a valid alternative (especially for crusted scabies) but contacts and environmental measures are still required; antihistamines alone for contacts is insufficient.
Only treating symptomatic contacts misses asymptomatic carriers who can reinfect the patient.
Permethrin must be applied to the entire body from neck to toes, not just affected areas.
clinicalPearl: All household and close contacts must be treated simultaneously on the same night as the index case to prevent scabies reinfection.
keyClue: Nocturnal itch, interdigital burrows, and multiple household contacts with similar symptoms
commonExamTrap: Treating only the symptomatic patient without addressing contacts, leading to treatment failure.
missedConcept: Scabies treatment fails when contacts are not treated simultaneously; the mite spreads via prolonged skin contact.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 10-week history of progressive proximal muscle weakness making it difficult to climb stairs or raise her arms above her head. She also has a violaceous rash on her eyelids (heliotrope rash), Gottron's papules over the metacarpophalangeal joints, and a V-sign rash on the upper chest. Her CK is markedly elevated at 4,200 U/L. EMG shows a myopathic pattern. Muscle biopsy confirms inflammatory myopathy. What must not be overlooked in the management of this patient?
options: Starting high-dose oral corticosteroids and discharging once CK normalises
Urgent age- and sex-appropriate malignancy screening, as dermatomyositis in adults carries a significantly increased risk of occult malignancy
Patch testing to identify any contact allergen triggering the skin rash
Empirical antibiotic therapy to cover for possible bacterial myositis
Advising the patient that the condition is benign and self-limiting in adults
explanation: Adult-onset dermatomyositis is strongly associated with underlying occult malignancy (especially ovarian, lung, colorectal, and breast cancers), with a peak risk in the first 3 years. All adults with new dermatomyositis must undergo comprehensive malignancy screening (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, gynaecological examination, tumour markers, colonoscopy as appropriate). Missing an underlying cancer is a serious management error.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Starting steroids is correct but discharging after CK normalises without malignancy screening is a critical omission.
Correct — malignancy screening is mandatory in adult dermatomyositis due to the high association with occult cancer.
Patch testing is irrelevant to dermatomyositis, which is an inflammatory myopathy not a contact condition.
This is an inflammatory (autoimmune) myopathy, not a bacterial infection; empirical antibiotics are inappropriate.
Dermatomyositis in adults is not benign or self-limiting; it carries significant morbidity, mortality, and malignancy risk.
clinicalPearl: Adult-onset dermatomyositis is a paraneoplastic phenomenon in up to 30% of cases — thorough malignancy screening at diagnosis is mandatory.
keyClue: 62-year-old woman with heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules, proximal myopathy, and elevated CK
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on the inflammatory myopathy and steroid treatment while failing to screen for occult malignancy.
missedConcept: Dermatomyositis in adults over 50 has a strong paraneoplastic association; malignancy must be actively excluded at presentation.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old sexually active woman presents with a 2-week history of an itchy rash over her trunk and proximal limbs. She recalls a single large oval scaly patch on her abdomen about 10 days before the rash appeared. On examination, there are multiple 1–2 cm oval scaly papules and plaques over the trunk following rib lines in a 'Christmas tree' pattern. The palms and soles are clear. She has no systemic symptoms. Which investigation must be performed to exclude the most important differential diagnosis?
options: Skin biopsy of the herald patch
Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titre
Serum RPR or TPHA (syphilis serology)
Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation for tinea
Parvovirus B19 IgM serology
explanation: Pityriasis rosea can be almost indistinguishable from the secondary syphilis rash, which also causes oval plaques on the trunk. However, secondary syphilis classically involves the palms and soles, and the sexual history makes syphilis a critical differential that must be excluded. Syphilis serology (RPR/VDRL confirmed by TPHA/FTA-ABS) is mandatory before diagnosing pityriasis rosea in a sexually active patient. Missing secondary syphilis has public health and clinical consequences.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Skin biopsy findings are non-specific and will not differentiate pityriasis rosea from secondary syphilis reliably.
ASO titres are used to investigate streptococcal infection and are not relevant here.
Correct — syphilis serology is mandatory as secondary syphilis is the most dangerous differential diagnosis to exclude in a sexually active patient.
KOH preparation can exclude tinea but cannot rule out syphilis, which is the critical differential.
Parvovirus B19 may cause a rash but is not the primary differential concern here given the pattern and sexual history.
clinicalPearl: Always exclude secondary syphilis with serology before diagnosing pityriasis rosea in a sexually active patient — both present with truncal oval scaly plaques.
keyClue: Sexually active young woman with Christmas tree pattern truncal rash and herald patch
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing pityriasis rosea without ordering syphilis serology, missing a treatable and notifiable infection.
missedConcept: Secondary syphilis is the most clinically important differential of pityriasis rosea; syphilis serology must always be checked.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 3-day history of fluid-filled blisters on his face and arms. The blisters rupture easily leaving a golden-crusted erythematous base. He attends a nursery. He is afebrile and systemically well. His mother reports that his 3-year-old sister developed a similar rash yesterday. On examination, there are multiple ruptured bullae with honey-coloured crust on the perioral area and arms. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral flucloxacillin for 5–7 days for both children and advise exclusion from nursery until the lesions have crusted over
Topical fusidic acid or mupirocin for 5–7 days for the index case, treat the sister similarly, and exclude both children from nursery until 48 hours after starting treatment
Reassure the mother that impetigo is self-limiting and advise saline soaks only
Systemic aciclovir as the vesicular rash may represent herpes simplex
Admit the child for IV antibiotics due to the risk of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
explanation: Localised non-bullous or bullous impetigo in a systemically well child is treated with topical antibiotics (fusidic acid or mupirocin). Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin or cefalexin) are reserved for widespread disease, recurrence, or failure of topical treatment. Both children should be treated and excluded from nursery until 48 hours after starting antibiotic treatment. Good hygiene measures (separate towels, handwashing) reduce spread.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral flucloxacillin is appropriate for extensive or systemic impetigo, but topical antibiotics are first-line for localised disease in a well child.
Correct — topical antibiotics for localised impetigo in a well child, with nursery exclusion for 48 hours after starting treatment.
Impetigo can spread and cause local complications; it is not managed with saline soaks alone.
The honey-coloured crust and the spread pattern are characteristic of impetigo (Staphylococcus aureus); aciclovir is not indicated.
Hospital admission is not required for localised, systemically well impetigo; monitoring for nephritis complications is appropriate but does not require admission.
clinicalPearl: Topical fusidic acid or mupirocin is first-line for localised impetigo in a well child; exclude from school/nursery for 48 hours after starting treatment.
keyClue: Honey-crusted ruptured bullae in a well child with spread to sibling
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral antibiotics as first-line when topical agents are sufficient for localised, systemically well impetigo.
missedConcept: Topical antibiotic therapy is adequate for localised impetigo; oral therapy is escalated for extensive or treatment-resistant disease.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old woman with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc) presents urgently with a 2-day history of severe right index finger pain. The fingertip is cyanotic and cool, and she has a small area of blackening at the tip suggesting impending digital gangrene. She is already on nifedipine 30 mg modified release. Her hands show skin tightening and sclerodactyly. ANA is positive with anti-centromere antibody. What is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Advise warming the hand and increasing nifedipine to 60 mg; review in one week
Stop nifedipine and switch to amlodipine
Urgent hospital admission for IV iloprost (prostacyclin analogue) infusion and vascular/rheumatology review
Apply topical glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) paste to the finger and discharge
Oral sildenafil and advise the patient to attend the emergency department only if the colour change worsens
explanation: This patient has a critical digital ischaemia/impending digital gangrene in systemic sclerosis — a vascular emergency. When digital ischaemia is severe or progressing despite oral calcium channel blockers, IV iloprost (a prostacyclin analogue) is the treatment of choice: it causes vasodilation and has antiplatelet effects. This requires hospital admission with specialist vascular and rheumatology input. Delay risks irreversible digital loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing nifedipine and reviewing in a week is insufficient when there is critical ischaemia and impending gangrene; urgent escalation is required.
Switching calcium channel blockers does not address the acute critical ischaemia requiring IV therapy.
Correct — IV iloprost with urgent specialist review is the appropriate escalation for critical digital ischaemia in systemic sclerosis.
Topical GTN may have a role as adjunctive therapy but is insufficient as the sole management for impending gangrene.
Oral sildenafil is used for maintenance/prevention of Raynaud's but is not sufficient for acute critical digital ischaemia; advising the patient to wait is dangerous.
clinicalPearl: IV iloprost is the treatment of choice for critical digital ischaemia in systemic sclerosis when oral therapy has failed — hospital admission is required.
keyClue: Impending digital gangrene (blackening fingertip) in a patient with systemic sclerosis already on nifedipine
commonExamTrap: Escalating oral medications rather than recognising that digital gangrene requires IV prostacyclin therapy and urgent specialist admission.
missedConcept: Critical digital ischaemia in systemic sclerosis is a vascular emergency requiring IV iloprost — oral therapy alone is insufficient at this stage.
redFlagReason: Impending digital gangrene risks irreversible digit loss; urgent IV prostacyclin therapy and specialist input are required without delay.
